results.possibleIdentity: Allan L. Griff (often spelled Allan Griff) is a veteran chemical engineer and consultant who specializes in plastics technology, specifically plastics extrusion. He graduated from Cornell University in 1955 with a Bachelor of Chemical Engineering (BChemEng) and began working as an independent consultant in 1961. Griff is the author of 'Plastics Extrusion Technology' (first published in 1962) and the 'Plastics Extrusion Operating Manual,' which has been updated annually since the 1980s. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was a leading technical expert in the plastics industry, a field that was of significant interest to artists collaborating with engineers through organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Beyond his engineering career, Griff has extensive interests in the arts and humanities; he has written over 300 poems and proverbs, participated in a cappella madrigal singing groups, and often speaks on the concept of 'cultural evolution.' His profile as an engineer with deep artistic interests and expertise in a revolutionary material of the 1960s (plastics) aligns with the technical advisors and collaborators who supported E.A.T. projects.

results.possibleIdentity: Rosalind Eichenstein (likely the individual referred to as Rosalind Etchewstein) is an art patron, researcher, and academic who was active in the New York art and counterculture scenes during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is particularly associated with Woodstock, New York, and is cited in historical contexts regarding the 'Sixties Counterculture' and its impact on that region. Professionally, she holds a PhD in social psychology and has co-authored scholarly research on topics such as gender differences in 'private wishes' (published in the journal Sex Roles in 1992). In the late 1980s, she was identified as an art patron attending contemporary art exhibitions in New York City, such as a British exhibit focused on the interaction between the observer and the artwork. She also served as a Senior Evaluation Consultant for the New York City Board of Education in the early 1990s. She was married to Robert Edelman and is the mother or stepmother of the artist and musician Nina Maudie Edelman.

results.possibleIdentity: Antonio da Silva (often cited as A. da Silva) was a Brazilian artist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was part of the circle of Brazilian artists living and working in New York during this period, alongside figures such as Hélio Oiticica and Lygia Pape. Historical records and research papers from the Latin American Art Forum indicate that he was associated with the 'Warhol crowd' and established contact with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. This circle of artists 'took advantage of the offerings' of E.A.T., which provided technological support and facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers for experimental projects. His name is sometimes grouped with other prominent Brazilian modernists and conceptualists of the era, such as Flavio Shiro Tanaka and Elisa Martins da Silveira Piani, in the context of the packaging and marketing of Latin American art in the United States during the late 1960s and 1970s. The name 'A. Dasilua' appears to be a phonetic transcription or typographical variation (likely due to OCR errors or historical document transcription) of his more common Portuguese name, Antonio da Silva.

results.possibleIdentity: Joseph Parker (1924–2016) was an American artist and restaurateur primarily known for his kinetic metal sculptures and whimsical cartoons. Born in Queens, New York, he served in the Navy during World War II before studying graphic design in Paris under Fernand Léger and Paul Colin via the G.I. Bill. Parker is best known for his 'drop-by-drop' steel welding technique used to create balanced, moving sculptures often compared to the work of Alexander Calder. In 1969, he and his wife, Sophie, founded the French restaurant 'Chez Sophie' in Hadley, New York, where his art and cartoons were frequently displayed. His work, which explored optical illusions and balanced movement, was the subject of various exhibitions throughout the 1960s and 1970s, including shows at Center Galleries in Albany and venues in Washington, D.C. His kinetic pieces are held in collections such as The Hyde Collection and the National Museum of Dance.

Joseph Parker (born 1930) is an American Postwar and Contemporary artist recognized as a pioneer or 'grandfather' of the Visionary Art movement. Active since the mid-1960s, he gained prominence during the counterculture era of the late 1960s and early 1970s for his kaleidoscopic, fluorescent paintings of sunsets and spiritual landscapes, which were frequently reproduced as blacklight posters. His work is characterized by metaphysical themes, often interpreting natural phenomena like sunrays as 'higher dimensional light beings.' His painting 'Sunrise with Columns' (1975) is part of the permanent collection of the Art Institute of Chicago. Associated with the California visionary scene, his work was featured in Walter Hopps' book 'Visions' and has been exhibited at contemporary venues like the Eric Firestone Gallery in New York.
